summary: Stancil argues that the early-2010s tech culture he experienced was not a temporary excess but a symptom of deeper structural incentives that breed toxicity. In contrast, the analytics community—exemplified by dbt’s Slack and Coalesce—has built a healthier, more inclusive culture, proving that tech’s ambition and impact can be separated from its worst behaviors. He concludes that software should learn from analytics by exporting this cultural model outward into the broader industry.
money_quotes: The analytics community’s success is proof that, even if software eats the world, software culture doesn’t have to.
It’s proof that the good things about the tech industry—the ambition, the impact, the financial opportunity—aren’t the causes of the bad.

Among all the characters in the community—the jesters, the cheerleaders, the philosophers and deep thinkers, the educators, the entertainers, the eager learners—one is conspicuously missing: the arrogant jerks.
Given how much the data industry tries to learn from software engineering, maybe it’s time to go the other direction.
There are a lot of dark spots in tech, and what a light, my lord, is needed to conquer so mighty a darkness.
key_insight: The analytics community demonstrates that tech’s cultural toxicity is not inevitable, and its healthier norms should be exported back into the broader software industry.
